did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0 architectures (CNNs), to classify melanoma skin cancer images. The study evaluated the impact of image pre-processing (skin hair removal and CLAHE) on the classification accuracy of these AI models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2024 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA). It details a specific methodology involving dataset collection (PH2, ISIC 2018, USK-Normal Skin), image pre-processing (skin hair removal, CLAHE), and model training using ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0 architectures. The authors report new quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.) and propose a method to bridge a knowledge gap, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Impact of Image Pre-Processing on Skin Cancer Classification Melanoma through Deep Learning
journal: 2024 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of image pre-processing on the classification of melanoma skin cancer using deep learning models. The researchers utilized the PH2, ISIC 2018, and USK-Normal Skin datasets, applying skin hair removal and CLAHE enhancement techniques to improve image quality. Two Convolutional Neural Network architectures, ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0, were trained and evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-score metrics. The results demonstrated that pre-processed images significantly improved classification performance compared to original images. Specifically, both ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0 achieved 100% accuracy after pre-processing, whereas they achieved 94% and 98.6% accuracy on original images, respectively. The study concludes that pre-processing is crucial for enhancing the accuracy and reliability of automated melanoma detection systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma from normal skin. It is described as a malignant tumor originating from melanocytes.
The comparator class in the binary classification task. Images from the USK-Normal Skin dataset were used as the non-melanoma class.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a category of skin cancer (benign) according to WHO classification, but not used as a label in the experiments.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a benign skin cancer category according to WHO classification, but not used as a label in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between melanoma and normal skin
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between 'melanoma' and 'normal skin' (non-melanoma) classes using ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0 architectures. The task is explicitly described as 'categorizing melanoma' and 'classifying melanoma skin disease and normal skin'. Metrics used include accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-score derived from a confusion matrix.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset used for melanoma skin images. Referenced in the paper as one of the sources for melanoma data.

use.training: Used as part of the melanoma class input for model training and testing.
use.testing: Used as part of the melanoma class input for model testing.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesion dataset from the ISIC Challenge 2018, used for melanoma skin images.

use.training: Used as part of the melanoma class input for model training and testing.
use.testing: Used as part of the melanoma class input for model testing.

datasets_used.name: USK-Normal Skin Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of normal skin images collected by the authors (Universitas Syiah Kuala), used as the non-melanoma class.

use.training: Used as the normal skin class input for model training and testing.
use.testing: Used as the normal skin class input for model testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-score metrics derived from confusion matrices, noting that pre-processed images yielded higher accuracy (100%) compared to original images (94% and 98.6%), but without statistical significance testing.

PH2_and_ISIC_2018_Melanoma_with_USK_Norm.description: Binary classification of melanoma (from PH2 and ISIC 2018) vs normal skin (from USK-Normal Skin). Results are reported for Original Images and Pre-processed Images (Skin Hair Removal + CLAHE).
PH2_and_ISIC_2018_Melanoma_with_USK_Norm.notes: The paper states that pre-processing (skin hair removal and CLAHE) significantly improved classification performance. Both models achieved 100% across all metrics after pre-processing. The EfficientNet-B0 architecture showed better performance than ResNet-34 on original images. Grad-CAM analysis confirmed that pre-processed images produced cleaner and more focused heatmaps compared to original images.

classification_or_output_head.output_activation: Not explicitly reported (standard for binary cross-entropy)

preprocessing_techniques: Skin hair removal using morphological features to recognize and remove hair contours, followed by inpainting techniques to restore lost details
CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) to enhance image sharpness and contrast, involving conversion to grayscale, tiling, histogram computation, and bilinear interpolation
Data augmentation including rotation (10 and 90 degrees), zoom (20 percent), shear (10 degrees), and horizontal/vertical flipping

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Roboflow was used to perform the augmentation.

conclusions: Image pre-processing, specifically skin hair removal combined with CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization), significantly improves the classification accuracy of melanoma skin cancer compared to using original images.

Both ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0 deep learning architectures achieved 100% accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-score when trained on pre-processed images, whereas they achieved 94% and 98.6% accuracy respectively on original images.
The EfficientNet-B0 architecture demonstrated superior performance compared to ResNet-34 when classifying original, unprocessed images.
Grad-CAM analysis confirmed that pre-processed images produce cleaner and more focused heatmaps, indicating that the model focuses on relevant skin features rather than artifacts like hair.
The study concludes that pre-processing is crucial for enhancing the accuracy and reliability of automated melanoma detection systems by removing interfering artifacts and enhancing image details.
important_remarks: The study reports 100% accuracy for both ResNet-34 and EfficientNet-B0 on pre-processed images, which is unusually high and suggests potential data leakage or overfitting, especially given the small dataset size (435 original images, augmented to 2200) and lack of external validation.

The normal skin class was sourced from a proprietary 'USK-Normal Skin Dataset' collected by the authors, while melanoma images came from PH2 and ISIC 2018; this mixed-source approach may introduce population bias and limits generalizability to other normal skin datasets.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported performance improvements, relying solely on point estimates from a confusion matrix.

The dataset is small (435 images before augmentation) and lacks diversity in skin types (PH2 and ISIC 2018 are predominantly Caucasian; USK-Normal Skin is from Indonesia), limiting clinical relevance for diverse global populations.
The paper does not report a separate test set or cross-validation strategy, raising concerns about data leakage during the training and evaluation phases.

The study is a conference paper with limited scope, focusing only on binary classification (melanoma vs. normal skin) and not addressing other common skin lesions (e.g., nevus, seborrheic keratosis) present in clinical practice.
Deployment concerns are acknowledged in the conclusion (edge AI, clinical workflow integration), but no technical details or feasibility studies for such deployment are provided.
